q: A pendulum with the time period of 1 s is losing energy due to damping. At a certain time, its energy is 45 J. If after completing 15 oscillations its energy has become 15 J, then its damping constant (in s-1) will be
question: A pendulum with the time period of 1 s is losing energy due to damping. At a certain time, its energy is 45 J. If after completing 15 oscillations its energy has become 15 J, then its damping constant (in s-1) will be
solution: Energy of a damping oscillator is <math><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-2</mo><mi>γ</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></msup></math> at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> where <math><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo></math>damping constant, <math><msup><mrow><mi>E</mi><mi>'</mi></mrow><mrow><mi> </mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msub><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>γ</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></msup></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>45</mn><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>γ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mn>15</mn></mfenced></mrow></msup></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>30</mn></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math>

q: A mass $M$, attached to a horizontal spring, executes S.H.M. with amplitude $A_1$. When the mass $M$ passes through its mean position then a smaller mass $\mathrm{m}$ is placed over it and both of them move together with amplitude $A_2$. The ratio of $\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)$ is :
question: A mass $M$, attached to a horizontal spring, executes S.H.M. with amplitude $A_1$. When the mass $M$ passes through its mean position then a smaller mass $\mathrm{m}$ is placed over it and both of them move together with amplitude $A_2$. The ratio of $\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)$ is :

subject: Physics
q: Two simple harmonic waves having equal amplitudes of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and equal frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> are moving along the same direction. The resultant amplitude is also <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The phase difference between the individual waves is _____ degree.
question: Two simple harmonic waves having equal amplitudes of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and equal frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> are moving along the same direction. The resultant amplitude is also <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The phase difference between the individual waves is _____ degree.
solution:
If two waves have the same amplitude, frequency, and direction, but are out of phase by ϕ degrees, then the amplitude of the resultant waves is given by AR=2AcosΔϕ2

Here, A1=A2=AR=8 cm

So, cosΔϕ2=12

Or phase difference between the individual waves is Δϕ2=60°⇒Δϕ=120°.

subject: Physics
q: A body is in simple harmonic motion with time period  <math> <mrow> <mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>s</mtext></mrow> </math> and amplitude <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>cm</mtext></mrow> </math>. Find the average velocity in the interval in which it moves from equilibrium position to half of its amplitude.
question: A body is in simple harmonic motion with time period  <math> <mrow> <mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>s</mtext></mrow> </math> and amplitude <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>cm</mtext></mrow> </math>. Find the average velocity in the interval in which it moves from equilibrium position to half of its amplitude.
solution:
∴x=Asin⁡ωt

A2=Asin⁡ωt

ωt=π6

t=π6ω

=π6×2π

t=T12

∴Time taken to reach from x=0 to x=A2 isT12

∴Average velocity=DisplacementTime

=A/2T/12

=AT×6

=6×10.5 

=12 cm/s

subject: Physics
q: The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by $x=10 \sin \left(w t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) m$. The time period of motion is $3.14 \mathrm{~s}$. The velocity of the particle at $t=0$ is ______$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by $x=10 \sin \left(w t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) m$. The time period of motion is $3.14 \mathrm{~s}$. The velocity of the particle at $t=0$ is ______$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
Given,
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}=3.14=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega} \\
& \omega=2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \mathrm{x}=10 \sin \left(\omega \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\
& \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=10 \omega \cos \left(\omega \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\
& \text { at } \mathrm{t}=0 \\
& \mathrm{v}=10 \omega \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=10 \times 2 \times \frac{1}{2}[\mathrm{using} \omega=2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}] \\
& \mathrm{v}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The metallic bob of simple pendulum has the relative density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>. The time period of this pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the metallic bob is immersed in water, then the new time period becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: The metallic bob of simple pendulum has the relative density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>. The time period of this pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the metallic bob is immersed in water, then the new time period becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
When the bob is immersed in water its effective weight is mg'=mg-FB, where, FB is buoyant force.

Then, g'=mg-FBm =ρBVg-ρWVgρBV

=ρB-ρwρBg =5-15×g=45g

Now, time period of simple pendulum is given by T=2πlg

Thus, the ratio T'T=gg'=g45g=54

⇒T'=T54=1025

Thus, the new time period is T'=55 s. 

Hence, value of x=5.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : A simple pendulum is taken to a planet of mass and radius, 4 times and 2 times, respectively, than the Earth. The time period of the pendulum remains same on earth and the planet.
Reason (R): The mass of the pendulum remains unchanged at Earth and the other planet.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : A simple pendulum is taken to a planet of mass and radius, 4 times and 2 times, respectively, than the Earth. The time period of the pendulum remains same on earth and the planet.
Reason (R): The mass of the pendulum remains unchanged at Earth and the other planet.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both $(\mathbf{A})$ and $(\mathbf{R})$ are true and $(\mathbf{R})$ is the correct explanation of $(\mathbf{A})$
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& g=\frac{G M}{R^2} \\
& g^{\prime}=\frac{G(4 M)}{(2 R)^2}=g
\end{aligned}$

A is correct, $R$ is correct ; but since $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{g}}$ doesn't depend on mass ; R doesn't explain A .

subject: Physics
q: A particle executes SHM of amplitude<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math>. The distance from the mean position when its kinetic energy becomes equal to its potential energy is:
question: A particle executes SHM of amplitude<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math>. The distance from the mean position when its kinetic energy becomes equal to its potential energy is:
solution:
The potential energy is given by 

U=mω2x22

The kinetic energy is given by K=mω2(A2-x2)2.

Using the condition that K=U,

12mω2A2-x2=12mω2x2

⇒x=A2

subject: Physics
q:
A light hollow cube of side length 10 cm and mass 10 g , is floating in water. It is pushed down and released to execute simple harmonic oscillations. The time period of oscillations is $y \pi \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}$, where the value of $y$ is
(Acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, density of water $=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ )
question:
A light hollow cube of side length 10 cm and mass 10 g , is floating in water. It is pushed down and released to execute simple harmonic oscillations. The time period of oscillations is $y \pi \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}$, where the value of $y$ is
(Acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, density of water $=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ )
solution:
Additional buoyant force
$\begin{aligned} & g \rho a^2 x=\sigma a^3 A \\ & A=\frac{\rho}{\sigma} \frac{g}{a} x \\ & T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma a}{\rho g}} \end{aligned}$ Now, $\sigma=\frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}}=10$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad T & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 0.1}{10^3 \times 10}} \\ & =2 \pi \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Using a simple pendulum experiment $g$ is determind by measuring its time period $T$. Which of the following plots represent the correct relation between the pendulum length $L$ and time period $T$ ?
question: Using a simple pendulum experiment $g$ is determind by measuring its time period $T$. Which of the following plots represent the correct relation between the pendulum length $L$ and time period $T$ ?
solution:
The time period $T$ of a simple pendulum of length $L$ is given by the formula:
$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$
Squaring both sides, we get:
$T^2 = 4\pi^2 \frac{L}{g}$
Rearranging the equation to find the relationship between $\frac{1}{T^2}$ and $L$:
$\frac{1}{T^2} = \frac{g}{4\pi^2 L}$
Let $y = \frac{1}{T^2}$ and $x = L$. The equation becomes:
$y = \frac{k}{x}$ where $k = \frac{g}{4\pi^2}$ is a constant.
This is the equation of a rectangular hyperbola in the first quadrant.
As $L$ increases, $\frac{1}{T^2}$ decreases non-linearly, approaching the axes asymptotically.
Comparing this with the given plots, option (2) correctly represents a hyperbolic relationship between $\frac{1}{T^2}$ and $L$.

If the length of the pendulum is reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>16</mn></mfrac></math> times of its initial value, the modified time period is

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves with simple harmonic motion in a straight line. In first <math> <mrow> <mi>τ</mi> <mo>s</mo> </mrow> </math>, after starting from rest it travels a distance a, and in next <math> <mrow> <mi>τ</mi> <mo>s</mo> </mrow> </math> it travels 2a, in same direction, then :
question: A particle moves with simple harmonic motion in a straight line. In first <math> <mrow> <mi>τ</mi> <mo>s</mo> </mrow> </math>, after starting from rest it travels a distance a, and in next <math> <mrow> <mi>τ</mi> <mo>s</mo> </mrow> </math> it travels 2a, in same direction, then :
solution:
x = - A cos ω t

- A + a = - A cos ω τ                ...(i)

- A + 3 a = - A cos ω 2 τ          ...(ii)

∴    - 1 + a A = - cos ω τ              ...(iii)

and - 1 + 3 a A = - cos 2 ω τ           ...(iv)

Adding (iii) and (iv)

2 = 3  cos ω τ - 2  cos 2 ω τ + 1

∴    2  cos 2 ω τ - 3  cos ω τ + 1 = 0

∴    cos ω τ = 1 2

∴        ω τ = π 3

∴         T = 6 τ

q: A particle is making simple harmonic motion along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis. If at a distances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> from the mean position the velocities of the particle are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively. The time period of its oscillation is given as:
question: A particle is making simple harmonic motion along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis. If at a distances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> from the mean position the velocities of the particle are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively. The time period of its oscillation is given as:
solution:
v2=ω2 A2-x2

A2=x12+v12ω2=x22+v22ω2

ω2=v22-v12x12-x22

T=2πx12-x22v22-v12

subject: Physics
q: The amplitude of a mass-spring system, which is executing simple harmonic motion decreases with time. If mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, Decay constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> then how much time is required for the amplitude of the system to drop to half of its initial value? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>693</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The amplitude of a mass-spring system, which is executing simple harmonic motion decreases with time. If mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, Decay constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> then how much time is required for the amplitude of the system to drop to half of its initial value? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>693</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
A=A0e-γt=A0e-bt2m

A02=A0e-bt2m

bt2m=ln2

t=2mbln2=2×500×0.69320

t=34.65 second.

subject: Physics
q:
A simple pendulum is placed at a place where its distance from the earth's surface is equal to the radius of the earth. If the length of the string is 4 m, then the time period of small oscillations will be _________s.

[take g=π2 m s-2]
question:
A simple pendulum is placed at a place where its distance from the earth's surface is equal to the radius of the earth. If the length of the string is 4 m, then the time period of small oscillations will be _________s.

[take g=π2 m s-2]
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at surface of earth, g=GMR2.

Now, at distance R from the surface of the earth, g'=GMR+R2=g4

Therefore,
T=2πlg4

⇒T=2π4×4 g

⇒T=2π4π=8 s

The spring is stiff enough so that it does not get distorted during the motion.
question: A mass $m=1.0 \mathrm{~kg}$ is put on a flat pan attached to a vertical spring fixed on the ground. The mass of the spring and the pan is negligible. solution: As $\mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{kx}$

q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: A second's pendulum has a time period of 1 second.

Statement II: It takes precisely one second to move between the two extreme positions. In the light of the above statements,

choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: A second's pendulum has a time period of 1 second.

Statement II: It takes precisely one second to move between the two extreme positions. In the light of the above statements,

choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are False
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
solution: Second pendulum has a time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> so statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> is false but from one extreme to other it takes only half the time period so statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> is true.

subject: Physics
q: A simple harmonic oscillator of angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is acted upon by an external force <math><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi> </mi><mi>N</mi><mo>. </mo></mrow></mrow></math>If the oscillator is at rest in its equilibrium position at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> its position at later times is proportional to:
question: A simple harmonic oscillator of angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is acted upon by an external force <math><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi> </mi><mi>N</mi><mo>. </mo></mrow></mrow></math>If the oscillator is at rest in its equilibrium position at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> its position at later times is proportional to:
solution:
It is given that oscillator at rest at t=0 i.e. at t=0, v=0. So, in option, we can check by putting v=dxdt=0.

(1) If x∝sin⁡t+12cos⁡2t

⟹v∝cos⁡t+12×2 (-sin⁡2t)

⟹  at t=0, v∝1-0≠0

(2) If x ∝cos⁡t-12sin⁡t

⟹  v∝-sin⁡t-12cos⁡t

⟹at t=0, v∝-12≠0

(3) If x∝sin⁡t-12 sin 2t

then υ∝cos⁡t-12×2cos⁡2t

⟹at t=0, v∝1-1=0

(4) If x∝sin⁡t+12sin⁡2t

⟹v∝cos⁡t+12×2cos⁡2t

⟹at t=0, v∝1+1

⟹  v∝2≠0

∴   in option 3  v=0   at   t=0

subject: Physics
q: Which graph represents the difference between total energy and potential energy of a particle executing SHM vs its distance from mean position?
question: Which graph represents the difference between total energy and potential energy of a particle executing SHM vs its distance from mean position?
solution:
In a SHM, mechanical energy is conserved. So, the sum of kinetic energy and potential energy remains constant.

For a SHM,

T.E.-U=K.E

⇒T.E.-U=12mv2=12mω2A2-x2

This is the equation of a downward parabola.

subject: Physics
q:
Two simple harmonic motions, as shown, are at right angles. They are combined to form Lissajous figures.
$x(t)=A \sin (a t+\delta)$
$y(t)=B \sin (b t)$
Identify the correct match below
question:
Two simple harmonic motions, as shown, are at right angles. They are combined to form Lissajous figures.
$x(t)=A \sin (a t+\delta)$
$y(t)=B \sin (b t)$
Identify the correct match below
solution:
From the two mutually perpendicular S.H.M.'s, the general equation of Lissajous figure,
$$
\frac{x^2}{A^2}+\frac{y^2}{B^2}-\frac{2 x y}{A B} \cos \delta=\sin ^2 \delta
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&x=A \sin (a t+\delta) \\
&y=B \sin (b t+r)
\end{aligned}
$$
Clearly $A \neq B$ hence ellipse.

question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion and it is located at <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi></math> and <math><mi>c</mi></math> at time <math><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> respectively. The frequency of the oscillation is:
solution:
a=Asin⁡ωt0...(1)

c=Asin⁡3ωt0...(3)

adding equation (1) and (3)

a+c=Asin⁡ωt0+sin⁡3ωt0=2Asin⁡2ωt0cos⁡ωt0...(4)

∵  sinC+sinD=  2sinC+D2cosC−D2

from equation (2) and (4)

a+cb=2cosωt0

ω= 1t0cos-1⁡a+c2b ⇒f=12πt0cos-1⁡a+c2b

subject: Physics
q: The displacement of a particle, executing simple harmonic motion with time period $T$, is expressed as $x(t)=A \sin \omega t$, where $A$ is the amplitude. The maximum value of potential energy of this oscillator is found at $t=T / 2 \beta$. The value of $\beta$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The displacement of a particle, executing simple harmonic motion with time period $T$, is expressed as $x(t)=A \sin \omega t$, where $A$ is the amplitude. The maximum value of potential energy of this oscillator is found at $t=T / 2 \beta$. The value of $\beta$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For SHM with $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, the potential energy is
$U = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2 = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 A^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$.
Maximum potential energy occurs when $\sin^2(\omega t) = 1$, i.e., when $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, ...$
The first maximum occurs at $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{2}$, giving $t = \frac{\pi}{2\omega} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{T}{2\pi} = \frac{T}{4}$.
Since this is given as $t = \frac{T}{2\beta}$.
We have $\frac{T}{4} = \frac{T}{2\beta}$, so $\beta = 2$.

subject: Physics
q:
A point mass oscillates along the $x$-axis according to the law $\mathrm{x=x_0 \cos (\omega t-\pi / 4)}$. If the acceleration of the particle is written as
$\mathrm{a=A \cos (\omega t+\delta)}$
question:
A point mass oscillates along the $x$-axis according to the law $\mathrm{x=x_0 \cos (\omega t-\pi / 4)}$. If the acceleration of the particle is written as
$\mathrm{a=A \cos (\omega t+\delta)}$
solution:
$\mathrm{v=-x_0 \omega \sin (\omega t-\pi / 4)}$
$\mathrm{a=-x_0 \omega^2 \cos \left(\omega t+\pi-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}$
$\mathrm{a=A \cos (\omega t+\delta)}$
$\mathrm{A=x_0 \omega^2 ; \quad \delta=\frac{3 \pi}{4}}$

q: The displacement of simple harmonic oscillator after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> seconds starting from its mean position is equal to half of its amplitude. The time period of harmonic motion is
question: The displacement of simple harmonic oscillator after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> seconds starting from its mean position is equal to half of its amplitude. The time period of harmonic motion is
solution:
Displacement equation is given by x=Asinωt

Now, when amplitude is halved,  A2=Asinωt

Or ωt=π6

At, t=3 s, ω3=π6⇒ω=π18

Time period, T=2πω=2ππ18=36 s

subject: Physics
q: In the figure given below. a block of mass M=490 g placed on a frictionless table is connected with two springs having same spring constant (K=2 N m-1). If the block is horizontally displaced through X m then the number of complete oscillations it will make in 14π seconds will be ______.
question: In the figure given below. a block of mass M=490 g placed on a frictionless table is connected with two springs having same spring constant (K=2 N m-1). If the block is horizontally displaced through X m then the number of complete oscillations it will make in 14π seconds will be ______.
solution:
Both the springs are connected in parallel, therefore

Keq=K+K=2K

Now, time period of spring-block system is given by,

T=2πmKeq=2πm2K

Given here, m=490 gm=0.49 kg and K=2 N m-1

So, T=2π0.492×2

=2π49400=2π720=7π10

Now, number of oscillation in 14π is

N=timeT=14π7π10=20

question: The maximum velocity of a particle, executing simple harmonic motion with an amplitude $7 \mathrm{~mm}$, is $4.4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The period of oscillation is
solution:
$A \omega=V_{\max }$
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{v}_{\max }}=0.01 \mathrm{sec}$

subject: Physics
q: Two simple pendulums of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are both given small displacement in the same direction at the same instant. They will be again in phase after the shorter pendulum has completed number of oscillations equal to:
question: Two simple pendulums of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are both given small displacement in the same direction at the same instant. They will be again in phase after the shorter pendulum has completed number of oscillations equal to:
solution:
Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time period of the two pendulums $\mathrm{T}_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{~g}}}$ and $\mathrm{T}_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4}{\mathrm{~g}}}$ As $\ell_1 < \ell_2$ therefore $\mathrm{T}_1 < \mathrm{T}_2$ Let at $\mathrm{t}=0$ they start swinging together. Since their time periods are different, the swinging will not be in unison always. Only when number of completed oscillations
differ by an integer, the two pendulums will again begin to swing together Let longer length pendulum complete $\mathrm{n}$ oscillation and shorter length pendulum complete $(n+1)$ oscillation. For unison swinging
$$
\begin{aligned}
& (n+1) T_1=n_2 \\
& (n+1) \times 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{g}}=(n) \times 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4}{g}} \\
& \Rightarrow n=1 \\
& \therefore n+1=1+1=2
\end{aligned}
$$
